TextENGNew: My mother was a very busy person.
changeReason: Changed 'woman' to 'person'; JPN 人 is gender-neutral

TextENGNew: She worked tirelessly, and each time her efforts were recognized, new doors opened for her.
Watching her climb ever higher, always radiant, she was my idol.
changeReason: Changed 'hero' to 'idol'; JPN 憧れ means object of admiration/aspiration rather than heroic figure

TextENGNew: Originally, my mother had been a designer at a small clothing manufacturer in Okinomiya.
changeReason: Changed 'clothing factory' to 'clothing manufacturer'; JPN 衣装メーカー more accurately translates as manufacturer/maker

TextENGNew: My father was a designer too.
They met at work and got married.
At first they lived in a small apartment in Okinomiya,@k but when my mother became pregnant with me, they moved to my grandmother's house in Hinamizawa—our current home.
We lived there until just before I started elementary school.
Looking back, those were the happiest days of my life.
Every day was happy, and I believed without a shred of doubt that tomorrow would bring the same happiness as today.
Happiness was so constant that I took it for granted—so much so that I didn't even feel it as happiness.
Every day was happy.
I was too young to really understand at the time, but apparently my parents' company had fallen into financial trouble.
Amid all that, my mother and several other young designers started talking about leaving the company and striking out on their own.
...Then things got complicated, and@k after one thing led to another, my mother and her group ended up being taken in by a more prominent design firm in Ibaraki.
I heard lots of words I didn't understand back then, like "business partnership" and "strategic alliance"—but what it all boiled down to was that we were moving to Ibaraki.
My mother and her fellow young designers were highly valued at the new company as experienced, talented assets who could hit the ground running.
But my father wasn't among them.
Unfortunately, my father didn't possess the same overflowing talent that my mother did.
So moving to Ibaraki also meant my father would have to give up being a designer.
The new company had high expectations for my mother and had promised her a position leading an important project.
She didn't want to let her abundant talent and passion go cold in a place like this rural village.
She said as much to persuade my father, and he—wanting to support her rise to even greater heights—agreed to quit his job and make the move.
For me at that age, moving meant losing every single one of my close friends.
I was young, and I cried my eyes out, refusing to go.
After the move, my mother's work at her new company blossomed brilliantly.
She'd always been the energetic, take-charge type who thrived in leadership roles, so I imagine she must have flourished to her heart's content in this new environment.
She came home late every night and seemed to have virtually no days off.
Whenever a project deadline approached, it wasn't unusual for her to stay overnight at the office.
But when she did come home, she always looked fulfilled, wearing a radiant smile I'd never once seen during our time in Hinamizawa.
She was so dazzling that I remember wishing I could shine like her someday when I grew up.
My father, on the other hand, wasn't faring so well.
He couldn't find any company willing to hire him as a designer, and ended up drifting from one small company to another, doing simple clerical work.
Everything he'd built up over the years was useless now. The move must have been painful for him too.
But he was always understanding of my mother.
As she was entrusted with ever more important projects and grew busier by the day, he took over the housework in her place.
He did his best with the cleaning, the laundry, and attending my school events.
Cooking was the one thing he simply couldn't manage—he could make rice, but the side dishes were always cold, store-bought fried foods from the deli.
The number of evenings when just my father and I sat at that table, eating dinner without my mother, kept growing.
Mom's busy again, huh.
Whenever I said that, my father would always reply: your mother is working incredibly hard.
So let's support her together, Reina.
That's what he always said.
I decided to help with the cooking.
Since Dad couldn't cook at all, I figured it would work out perfectly if I got good at it.
Whenever Mom was in the kitchen, I'd eagerly help out and learn her recipes.
She apologized for rarely having time to cook for us, but she seemed happy that I was carrying on her cooking tradition.
I think I wrote in a school essay around that time that my dream was to become a clothing designer like my mother.
changeReason: Changed 'school yearbook' to 'school essay'; JPN 文集 refers to a collected essay anthology, not a yearbook

TextENGNew: But... it seemed I lacked my mother's extraordinary sense of style.
I'd often heard her say that taste isn't something you learn—it's something you're born with.
...So I remember feeling dejected, realizing I could never match my mother as a designer.
That's why I decided that rather than trying to become like her, I'd try to get closer to her by filling in the things she lacked.
I wanted to master not just cooking, but all the housework—cleaning, laundry, grocery shopping, everything.
My father, who was hopeless at housework, was thrilled that I could step in and handle things so capably.
Unable to find work and spending a lot of time at home, he seemed to treasure the time spent doing chores together with his daughter more than anything.
I became more and more capable of standing in for my mother,@k while she grew so busy with her increasingly responsible positions that she couldn't do any housework at all.
As if to make up for leaving me alone so much, my mother occasionally took me out to have fun.
She'd say things like "let's go eat something delicious—don't tell your father" and take me out on little outings.
We'd go to fancy restaurants or amusement parks.
Sometimes it was just the two of us, and sometimes her work colleagues came along.
Once, she took me to a summer beach barbecue with people from her office.
I was introduced as her daughter, and everyone fussed over me.
I especially remember this one guy—a good-looking young man with a tan and dyed hair (he got mad when I called him "uncle")—who was particularly sweet to me.
He often came along when my mother and I went out together.
Even on our outings, my mother frequently made work calls or combined our trips with work errands,@k so having someone from her office along didn't strike me as unusual.
But I was clingy and preferred having my mother to myself, so honestly, I was happier when the work people didn't come.
That day, too, my mother took me out to a trendy shopping district.
She always indulged me whenever we went out together.
She'd buy me anything without hesitation and treat me to whatever I wanted.
That day, she was especially generous.
She led me into one shop after another—boutiques and accessory stores, the kind of places I'd never dare enter on my own.
I tried to decline, saying things wouldn't suit me, but she bought everything without a second thought and gave them to me as presents.
I remember the price tags had a lot of zeros.
That day, for the first time, I realized that my mother wasn't just successful as a designer—she had a great deal of money.
Then we stopped to rest at an upscale café where even an orange juice cost 1,000 yen.
My mother clapped her hands together, smiled at me, and spoke.
"How would you like to make your dream come true, Reina?" she asked.
Back in kindergarten, when we'd been given the assignment to draw a picture of our dream, I'd drawn myself eating heaping piles of fruit parfait all to myself.
She was saying she'd make that dream come true for me, right here and now.
It was just an ordinary day.
There was nothing special about it—it wasn't my birthday or anything.
But my mother was always lavishly generous when she took me out, so I didn't find it strange at all and accepted with pure delight.
Five, six fruit parfaits were lined up before me.
I remember it felt like the world I'd scrawled in crayon on that drawing paper had come to life—a fluffy, dreamlike world.
There was no way we could finish them all,@k but Mom and I dug into those seemingly endless parfaits together, laughing and playing with them like kids in a sandbox.
Getting full on nothing but fruit parfait was a first-in-my-life experience, and I remember being wrapped in an overwhelming sense of happiness.
Savoring that happiness, we chatted away about nothing in particular.
``Reina-chan.@k@vS09/00/173000002. Do you remember the person who came with us when we went to the movies and dinner the other day?``
``...Umm, Uncle Akihito?@k@vS09/02/170200203. Hau, but I'll get smacked if I don't call him "big brother"...``
``Hahahaha.@k@vS09/00/173000004. Reina-chan, do you like Akihito-san?``
``I hate him!!@k@vS09/02/170200205. He's always teasing me.``
``That's because he adores you.@k@vS09/00/173000006. You two got along so well at the barbecue.``
``Hmmmm.@k@vS09/02/170200207. We get along sometimes, I guess.@k@vS09/02/170200208. ...I guess.``
My mother watched me and laughed softly.
I didn't understand why she'd suddenly brought up Uncle Akihito, but I was too young to think deeply about it.
Looking back, I think the signs were there even then.
I just didn't recognize that feeling for what it was.
``Hey.@k@vS09/00/173000008. Reina-chan, who do you like better—Mommy or Daddy?``
changeReason: Changed to child-register 'Daddy' matching JPN お父さん; preserves childlike perspective

TextENGNew: ``Mommy.``
changeReason: Changed to child-register 'Mommy' matching JPN お母さん; preserves the intimate mother-daughter tone

TextENGNew: It wasn't that I disliked my father, but after being showered with this much generosity, answering "Mommy" felt only natural.
changeReason: Changed to child-register 'Mommy' matching JPN お母さん; preserves the intimate mother-daughter tone

TextENGNew: ``If Mommy and Daddy were going to live in separate houses, which house would you want to live in?``
changeReason: Changed to child-register 'Mommy' matching JPN お母さん; preserves the intimate mother-daughter tone

TextENGNew: ``You see, Mommy is...@k@vS09/00/173000011. thinking about leaving Daddy and marrying Akihito-san.``
changeReason: Changed to child-register 'Mommy' matching JPN お母さん; preserves the intimate mother-daughter tone

TextENGNew: By that point, my mother had achieved considerable success.
I didn't know it at the time, but she'd already been headhunted by an even larger company, rising to a position of still greater responsibility where she could fully exercise her talents.
And it wasn't just her position.
Unlike my father, who could only bring in a part-time worker's salary, my mother earned more than enough to support a family of three with plenty to spare.
I'd more or less sensed that from how freely she spent money.
The more my mother succeeded, the higher she climbed, and the more she surrounded herself with people who matched her standing.
All of them were brilliantly talented and shining.
Compared to them, my father may have looked faded in her eyes.
It was true that I liked Uncle Akihito.
But I liked him in the category of "other people"—it wasn't even on the same level as my father.
For me, who loved both my father and my mother, hearing that she wanted to leave him felt like the ground splitting open beneath my feet.
And I quickly realized just how fitting that expression was.
Because I had to choose which side of that rift to stand on.
My mother told me to come live with her at her new house.
Her "new house" was a house with a stranger named Uncle Akihito in it.
changeReason: Added 'stranger' characterization matching JPN 他人 (outsider); emphasizes perception of Uncle Akihito as interloper

TextENGNew: I had believed that a home and family were sacred.
I had believed they were holy ground that no outsider could tread upon.
...And that was exactly why I couldn't believe my mother would so casually let an outsider into that sacred space.
Why did you stop loving Dad?
My mother didn't answer. She looked down slightly.
I pressed her further.
Hey, why can't you stay with Dad?
That question, I think, cut deeper into my mother's heart than anything else I could have said.
To put it bluntly—this was my mother's affair.
What other way is there to say it, other than that she'd fallen for a man more appealing than my father?
I knew better than anyone that my father had done nothing wrong.
The move from Hinamizawa was supposed to have been decided out of a desire to support my mother as much as possible.
Nothing good had come of it for him personally.
In truth, I think he'd had an incredibly hard time ever since we moved.
Yet he'd worked hard in quiet, unnoticed ways, hoping to at least be her support from the shadows.
Hadn't my mother been grateful for his devoted support behind the scenes?
So.@k ...I saw this as a betrayal.
I told her I refused everything—the divorce included.
So loudly that every head in the restaurant turned.
Just keep being friends with Uncle Akihito like before.
changeReason: Changed 'continue your relationship' to 'keep being friends'; reframed through childlike perspective matching JPN context

TextENGNew: I feel sorry for Dad.
This isn't fair to Dad at all.
Dad has done nothing but support you from the shadows all this time. How can you do something this cruel to him?
I screamed with every ounce of my voice.
But my mother said—
...it's too late.
Why?!@k You haven't told Dad yet, have you? Then it's not too late!
``You see, Mommy is...@k@|@y@vS09/00/173000013. ...pregnant.``
changeReason: Changed to child-register 'Mommy' matching JPN お母さん; preserves the intimate mother-daughter tone

TextENGNew: I don't know whether my mother ever told my father about the divorce face to face.
All I know is that a middle-aged woman who introduced herself as my mother's attorney appeared, and I saw her speaking with my father at the door and on the phone several times.
My father begged the attorney again and again to let him speak with his wife directly, but it seemed that was never granted.
From those exchanges, I could tell that the divorce had come completely out of the blue for him.
The attorney imposed restriction after restriction on my father—no direct meetings, no contacting her workplace, and everything else.@k In the end, she told him never to see my mother again.
Still desperate to talk to her, he contacted mutual friends, but all he learned was the name of the man she was having an affair with.
My father asked me if I knew the man.@k I told him I did.
I said I knew him.
A funny man who always gave me candy.@k That's what I said.
...I will never forget the look on my father's face at that moment.
He struck me for the first time in my life.
Hard enough that the mark of his palm stayed on my cheek for a while.@k Then he collapsed to the floor and sobbed.
After a moment, I started crying too, as if pulled along. ...Not because he'd hit me.
Uncle Akihito had been kind to me, so I'd returned his kindness with friendliness.
...No—more than that, I'd believed that a girl should be kind to everyone.@k ...That's what I'd thought.
But I'd been wrong.
I learned that in this world, there are many people you should be kind to—and then there are "enemies" you should never have shown kindness to at all.
"Enemies" are people whose very existence threatens to destroy your life.
Whether they bear malice or not is beside the point.
They're like weeds sprouting unbidden in a flowerbed.
A dandelion may be blameless, but because it steals nutrients meant for the tulips, it has to be pulled out.
Even though the dandelion did nothing wrong.@k ...That's what an "enemy" is.@k A bad person.
Yes, that man had been a bad person.
I should never have accepted him.
I should have rejected him with everything I had.
If I had, maybe I could have kept some distance between him and my mother.
If I had, maybe I could have kept my mother from feeling that things could work out with that man.
If that had been the case... then maybe, through my own efforts, my parents' marriage could have been saved.
My father and I cried together... but our tears had slightly different meanings.
For my father, they must have been tears at the sheer suddenness and unfairness of it all.
But for me,@k they were tears of regret—regret that I'd had chances to choose differently, chances to prevent this day, and I'd let them all slip by.
My father stroked my cheek, opened his mouth to say he was sorry... but couldn't even manage that, and simply cried with me.
Some time later, the attorney arranged for me—and only me—to see my mother.
My mother invited me to come live with her.
I didn't know what to say. I just sat there looking down, listening to her apologies.
My father knew I was going to see her that day.
So he'd entrusted me with a letter, asking me to give it to her.
I don't know what he wrote.
But even just feeling the letter through the envelope, I could sense its texture, and somehow I could imagine what was written inside.
...Because the letter inside felt rough and warped.
The tears my father had shed onto the paper must have dried and buckled its surface.
My mother took the letter, said she'd read it later, and was about to put it in her shoulder bag.
I stopped her and asked her to read it right there.
These were the last words my father could entrust to her.@k I wanted to see how she would receive them.
But she didn't read it.
She said she wanted to read it alone later, and put it in her bag.
In that moment, I understood.
...No, "understood" is too vague.
It was so blindingly clear that I should say I knew.
I knew that my mother had lied.
She had no intention whatsoever of reading my father's letter.
The moment I was out of sight, she'd throw it away. She'd only lied because she wanted to look good in front of me.
The subtle twist of her eyebrows and the corners of her mouth told me so more clearly than words ever could.
In that instant, I finally understood something else.
The man who had seduced my mother wasn't the only one at fault.
My mother herself was a bad person too.
From that moment on,
my mother's existence vanished from the world centered around me—dissolved into nothing, like dry ice sublimating without a trace.
There was no more agonizing over which parent to live with.
``I won't go to your house.@k@vS09/02/170200213. My home is the Ryuugu house.@k@vS09/02/170200215. I don't want to see you anymore.``
changeReason: Changed 'I don't want to go' to 'I won't go'; JPN 行きません is a firm refusal, not a statement of preference

TextENGNew: ``But Reina, if you stay with your father, your life from now on...``
At that moment, for the first time in my life, I felt revulsion at my own name.
``Don't.@k@vS09/02/170200217. Don't you dare call me Reina so casually.@k@vS09/02/170200218. ...Excuse me.@k@vS09/02/170200219. Goodbye.``
changeReason: Added 'casually' matching JPN 気安く; changes from blanket rejection to rebuke of presumed familiarity

TextENGNew: Those were my last words to my mother... no, to the woman who had once been my mother.
After that, days that felt both endlessly long and impossibly short continued—days devoid of any sense of the passing seasons.
Life without my mother in the house shouldn't have been anything unusual.
...But every trace of her that remained—@k her toothbrush, her mug, her favorite books—every time those things caught our eyes, my father wept, and I seethed.
That's why I decided to erase every last trace of her from the house.

I started stuffing her things roughly into black garbage bags.@k My movements grew more and more violent,@k and by the time I'd knocked over her wardrobe, smashed her music box, and shattered her vanity mirror...... my memories became hazy.
...When I try to remember anything beyond that point... my mind goes foggy.
That's because the medication the doctor gave me back then always left me feeling dull and listless whenever I took it.
That sluggishness must have left a deep impression,@k because whenever I try to recall those days, I'm invariably hit with the same dull, lethargic feeling the pills used to give me...
...I destroyed everything that carried my mother's scent.@k But the thing I most wanted to destroy may have been myself.
Even after erasing every trace of her, the regret that wouldn't fade was ultimately turned inward, upon myself.
Or perhaps I wanted to smear mud on my mother's success-adorned life by defiling myself—her own daughter.
At that very, very last moment. The absolute last.
Oyashiro-sama came to me.
Oyashiro-sama told me:@k go back to Hinamizawa.
Yes. This had all been a curse.
We had broken Oyashiro-sama's decree that one must never abandon Hinamizawa, and this was divine punishment upon the Ryuugu family.
Once I realized that, ...everything—all the bizarre, incomprehensible things that had happened since my mother left—suddenly made sense.
Oyashiro-sama's curse had been raining down upon us.
It was Oyashiro-sama's call, telling us to hurry back to Hinamizawa.
That's right.@k We should have stayed in Hinamizawa all along.@k Everything went wrong because we moved.@k We had to go back to Hinamizawa.
...My mind grew foggy... and a dull ache throbbed through it.
Perhaps it was a cry from within myself, telling me not to force these memories any further.
The house where we'd once lived in Hinamizawa was still standing.
My grandmother had already passed away, and it was said to be the only property she'd left behind.
I remembered having lived in Hinamizawa, but my memories of a village I'd left just before starting elementary school had faded considerably.
All I vaguely recalled was the atmosphere.
I couldn't remember the roads or the layout at all, nor could I recall the names of the friends I'd played with so closely back then.
The neighbors understood that the deceased grandmother's son had come back divorced,@k but that didn't mean they knew us, and naturally, I didn't know them either.
It was almost like starting over in a completely new place.
...Even so.
To me, this was the hometown I was meant to return to.
Compared to Ibaraki, everything was rural and inconvenient, and both my father and I struggled to adjust.
But the old house, now renovated, was a home for just me and my father.
Our new life there seemed to help him forget the grief of the divorce.
And with Mii-chan being so kind to me, I gradually settled into my new life too.
I faithfully filled my mother's role, and working in tandem with my father, little by little... we began to reclaim our happy days.
Anyone can live happily.
"Born under an unlucky star"—that's just an excuse made by people too lazy to put in the effort to be happy.
I refuse to surrender to an unhappy fate.
I will absolutely, without fail, reclaim those lost days—those happy days.@k ...Days so saturated with happiness that you forget to even notice it.
I won't cry anymore.@k I refuse to cry.@k I used up every last tear on the night my father struck my cheek.
The curse of Oyashiro-sama is over, because we came back to Hinamizawa.
So from now on, I'm going to be happy...
Just then, pooled rainwater cascaded off in a rush, striking the car's roof with a loud clatter, startling me back to my senses.
Outside, it was still raining.
Before I'd noticed, it had gone completely dark.
The shadows cast by the desk lamp were deeper and blacker than ink—as if nothing existed within them.
Then... if I turned off this light... could I erase the whole world?
I turned off... the light.
I was swallowed by pitch-black darkness.
But the chill of the air and the sound of rain remained utterly unchanged, matter-of-factly reminding me that I still existed in this world.
The curse of Oyashiro-sama, huh.
Did the curse of Oyashiro-sama drive my family to ruin?
Or was it just a convenient excuse I'd invented—someone other than myself to blame—because I didn't want to feel responsible for the divorce?@k ...Sometimes I wasn't sure.
Oyashiro-sama hasn't visited me since that time.
...Sometimes I wonder if it was all just a delusion born from my broken mind.
But... he really did come, back then.
Of that much, I'm certain.
...Right, Oyashiro-sama?
I left Hinamizawa and became unhappy. Then I came back and found happiness again. I'm grateful.
But.
If I've become unhappy within Hinamizawa itself... then where am I supposed to go this time...?
changeReason: Reframed as conditional 'If I've become unhappy within Hinamizawa itself'; adds existential weight matching JPN 雛見沢の中で不幸になった

TextENGNew: ``Stop it, Rena...@k@vS09/02/170200221. ...Don't think about being unhappy.@k@vS09/02/170200222. I am happy.@k@vS09/02/170200223. I'm going to be happy.@k@vS09/02/170200224. So... I can definitely become happy...!``
If I let this darkness swallow me, I'd never be able to find happiness again.
That sudden fear gripped me. I fumbled for the desk lamp's switch and turned the light back on.
When I looked at the clock, it was well past 11 PM.
I needed to head home soon, or I wouldn't have time to prepare tomorrow's lunch box.@k At the very least, I had to set the timer on the rice cooker.
The rain didn't look like it was going to let up.
...Thinking that, I peered through the window, but all I could see was my own face reflected back. The outside was completely invisible.
I spoke to the reflection of myself in the glass.
It's a wonderful thing that Dad looks happy.
...After all, for the longest time, he'd been like a dead man.
The fact that he'd gotten his smile back is proof that we're reclaiming our happy life.
After all... the reason Dad suffered so deeply was... because of me.
So it's only natural that I take care of things until he gets his smile back and we become truly happy.
If I spend time here by myself, it's probably less of a bother for Dad too,@k ...and I don't have to force a smile I don't feel like making.
But then,@k ...the me in the glass whispered back.
Rena, you really are such a good girl.
...Loving everyone equally, being kind to all.@k You can make anyone like you.
But, hey. Did you forget...?
In this world, there are many people you should be kind to...@k and "enemies" you have to reject with everything you've got. Didn't you learn that lesson...?
No. Don't listen to her.
...The me in the glass is always so cruel.
Harsh words, always mocking my struggles.
...And yet, she always says the things I want to say but can't bring myself to...
Let's go home, Rena.@k To your house.
Forget about the cruel Rena in the mirror.
Now that I think about it, I'm a bit hungry.
After eating that much... I still get hungry, huh.
Oh right.@k ...Rina-san bought me a shortcake.
I've never had a shortcake from Angel Mort, but I bet it's delicious.
I should thank Rina-san the next time I see her. Tell her it was delicious.
...Rina-san's way of speaking may betray her upbringing, but I'm sure she's not a bad person.
changeReason: Changed 'she's a good person' to 'she's not a bad person'; JPN 悪い人じゃない is a litotes (not bad) expressing hedged judgment, which is significantly different from an affirmative 'good'

TextENGNew: After all, she gets along so well with Dad.
The smile I could never manage to draw from my father no matter how hard I tried—Rina-san coaxed it out of him so effortlessly.
I should be grateful to Rina-san.
I should thank her for cheering up my father.
But... I don't know why.
...Her perfume... I just can't bring myself to like it...
